coredata.dc:title: Three-dimensional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide composite hydrogels as binder-free electrodes for symmetric supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Wenhui
Wang, Jiankai
Wu, Tao
Wen, Jing
Zhao, Baoru
Wei, Liang
Zhang, Yong
Fan, Shan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide composite hydrogels (N-GQDs/rGOs) were prepared via a hydrothermal approach from graphene oxide (GO) aqueous solution. During the preparation of the samples, ammonia water was used as reducing agent, pH regulator and nitrogen dopant, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as spacer between graphene sheets. Benefiting from their three-dimensional porous structures, large specific surface area, large number of heteroatom functional groups (N, O) on the surface, and the synergistic effect between GQDs and graphene sheets, the synthesized samples exhibit excellent electrochemical performance. Particularly, the N-GQDs/rGO-20 sample based binder-free symmetric supercapacitor show a high specific capacitance of 277.8 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1, and this value can still maintain at 220.9 F g−1 even at 10 A g−1 in 6 M KOH electrolyte. Moreover, the capacitance retention rate of the symmetric supercapacitor can reach 92.7% after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1, indicating its excellent cycle stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen doping
3D porous structure
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, (b) Raman and (c) FT-IR of GO, rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs.

(a) XPS survey spectra of GO, rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs, (b) O1s XPS spectra of GO, (c) O1s XPS spectra of rGO (d) O1s XPS spectra of N-GQDs/rGO-20, (e) C1s XPS spectra of N-GQDs/rGO-20, (f) N1s XPS spectra of N-GQDs/rGO-20.
BET adsorption-desorption curves (a) and pore size distributions (b) of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs.
SEM images of (a) rGO, (b) N-GQDs/rGO-10, (c) N-GQDs/rGO-20, (d) N-GQDs/rGO-30.
TEM images of (a) GQDs, (b) Histogram of the size distribution of GQDs, (c) rGO, (d) N-GQDs/rGO-20.

Electrochemical performance of the symmetric supercapacitors: CV curves of (a) N-GQDs/rGO-20 at different scan rate, (b) rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs at 50 mV s−1 and GCD curves of (c) N-GQDs/rGO-20 at different current densities, (d) rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs at 1 A g−1.

(a) Specific capacitances measured at different current densities, (b) Ragone plots, (c) EIS results of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs. Inset in (c) shows the expanded high-frequency region of the plots, (d) Long-term cycling stability of N-GQDs/rGO-20 measured at 10 A g−1. Inset in (d) shows CV curves of N-GQDs/rGO-20 at 50 mV s−1 before and after 10,000 cycles.
Illustration of the formation process of the N-GQDs/rGOs samples.
Raman peaks and ID/IG ratios of GO, rGO and N-GQDS/rGOs.
The elemental contents and C/O ratios obtained by XPS analysis of all samples (at.%).
Summary of BET, total pore volume and most probable pore size of all samples.

head.ce:title: Three-dimensional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide composite hydrogels as binder-free electrodes for symmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Graphene Functional Materials Research Laboratory, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Graphene Functional Materials Research Laboratory, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide composite hydrogels (N-GQDs/rGOs) were prepared via a hydrothermal approach from graphene oxide (GO) aqueous solution. During the preparation of the samples, ammonia water was used as reducing agent, pH regulator and nitrogen dopant, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as spacer between graphene sheets. Benefiting from their three-dimensional porous structures, large specific surface area, large number of heteroatom functional groups (N, O) on the surface, and the synergistic effect between GQDs and graphene sheets, the synthesized samples exhibit excellent electrochemical performance. Particularly, the N-GQDs/rGO-20 sample based binder-free symmetric supercapacitor show a high specific capacitance of 277.8 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1, and this value can still maintain at 220.9 F g−1 even at 10 A g−1 in 6 M KOH electrolyte. Moreover, the capacitance retention rate of the symmetric supercapacitor can reach 92.7% after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1, indicating its excellent cycle stability.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid expansion of global economy, human's request for energy is increasing day by day. The development and application of new energy have become an urgent problem for people to solve [1–3]. However, when the current generated by various new energy sources (wind energy, solar energy, tidal energy, etc.) is connected to the power system, due to its inherent instability (dispersion, volatility, intermittency, etc.), the traditional energy storage device cannot meet the requirements of use, resulting in the low utilization rate of new energy [4–6]. The emergence of supercapacitors successfully solved the above problems. Supercapacitors are a new type of energy storage device between traditional capacitors and chemical batteries, which has the features of high output power, fast charging speed, long cycle life and environmental protection. Therefore, it has great application prospects in aerospace, mobile communication, electric vehicle and other fields [7–9].

The performance of supercapacitors is mainly defined by the electrode materials. According to the energy storage mechanisms, the electrode materials of supercapacitors can be divided into two categories: electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) electrode materials and pseudocapacitance materials. EDLC electrode materials are generally carbon materials (graphene, carbon nanotube, activated carbon and carbon fiber, etc.) with high specific surface area and stable chemical properties. Carbon electrode materials have long service life, but have relatively low specific capacitance and energy density. Pseudocapacitance materials refer to transition metal oxides or conductive polymers that can undergo redox reactions with electrolytes. This type of material usually has high specific capacitance and energy density, but their cycle stability is poor, and cannot meet the requirements for the supercapacitors in practical applications [10]. Therefore, the development of carbon electrode materials with good performance has become the research focus and hope for improving the performance of supercapacitors.

Graphene has exceptional physical and chemical behaviors, such as superb electrical conductivity, outstanding thermal conductivity, and ultra-high mechanical strength, etc. Consequently, it has been extensively applied in the field of energy storage devices [11,12]. When pristine graphene is used as electrode material, severe sheets agglomeration often occurs, resulting in the low utilization rate of its specific surface, which seriously damages its electrochemical performance. In order to solve this problem, researchers have reported many methods, such as nitrogen doping or synthesis of graphene-based pseudocapacitance composites [13–15]. The latest research results show that adding spacer between graphene sheets to prevent the agglomeration and stacking of graphene materials has become an efficient method to boost their electrochemical performance [16,17].

GQDs can usually be defined as graphene dots with a size of less than 10 nm and a thickness of less than 10 layers, which have good electrical conductivity (50 S m−1) and high specific surface area (1502 m2 g−1) [18–20]. After being composited with other materials, the structural stability and conductivity of the material are both can be enhanced [21]. GQDs prepared by using GO have graphene structure and also contain substantial oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups at the edges, which makes it has excellent dispersibility in water. These characteristics make GQDs an excellent candidate material for graphene interlayer spacer. Moreover, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the edge of GQDs can also generate pseudocapacitance through the redox reactions, thereby enhancing the electrochemical performance of the materials [22]. However, there are few reports on the use of GQDs as electrode materials for supercapacitors.

Compared with other reducing agents, ammonia water as the reducing agent of GO has the advantages of low toxicity, strong pH adjustment ability, and easy to mix with GO solution. In addition, ammonia water as a nitrogen dopant for graphene not only has a wide source and low cost, but also has a simple molecular structure and strong activity, which makes it easy to achieve nitrogen doping on graphene sheets [23].

In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide composite hydrogels (N-GQDs/rGOs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using GO as the precursor, ammonia water as the reducing agent, nitrogen dopant and pH regulator, and GQDs as the spacer of graphene sheets (Scheme 1 ). In response, we suppose that the prepared N-GQDs/rGOs samples should have substantial nitrogen and oxygen functional groups, 3D porous structure, good electrical conductivity and high specific surface area. Moreover, the N-GQDs/rGOs samples will have excellent wettability and good pseudocapacitance activity under the action of copious heteroatomic functional groups. Furthermore, GQDs can not only enhance the porous structure of the sample, but also increase the specific capacitance of the material through the heteroatomic functional groups on its edge and its synergistic interaction with graphene sheets. Hence, we suppose that the N-GQDs/rGOs samples will present good electrochemical properties.

The GO utilized in this paper was synthesized via a Hummers' method [24]. GQDs was prepared by an easy top-down technique. First, 160 mg GO was dispersed in 80 mL DI water, and obtain a uniform GO dispersion after ultrasonic treatment. Then, the GO solution and 160 mL of hydrogen peroxide were added to a 500 mL flask and refluxed at 95 °C for 12 h to obtain a bright yellow GQDs solution. Subsequently, the solution was placed in a rotary evaporator at 60 °C and rotary evaporated to 2 mL, and finally freeze-dried to obtain 120 mg of GQDs. Therefore, the concentration of GQDs solution prepared by this method is 0.5 mg mL−1.

N-GQDs/rGOs samples were prepared by hydrothermal method. In case of N-GQDs/rGO-10, 66.7 mL 2 mg mL−1 GO solution (133.4 mg) and 26.6 mL 0.5 mg mL−1 GQDs solution (13.3 mg) were added to a 200 mL beaker and treated with ultrasound for 30 min. Subsequently, ammonia water was added to the beaker to regulate the pH value of the mixed solution to 10. Finally, the mixed solution was sonicated for 60 min and then added to the reaction kettle and then kept at 170 °C for 12 h to obtain composite hydrogel. The prepared hydrogel was washed three times with deionized water and then sealed in a beaker for later use. In the preparation process of N-GQDs/rGO-20 and N-GQDs/rGO-30, the dosage of GO was 66.7 mL (133.4 mg), while the dosage of GQDs was 13.3 mL (6.65 mg) and 8.9 mL (4.45 mg), respectively. The N-GQDs/rGO-10, N-GQDs/rGO-20 and N-GQDs/rGO-30 were nominated according to the mass ratio of GO to GQDs. In contrast, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) hydrogel was synthesized without the addition of ammonia water and GQDs solution at the same condition.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) results were obtained utilizing a D8-FOCUS diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Raman characterization was executed on an Invia Raman microscope. FTIR analysis was performed on an AVATAR-370 spectrometer. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was carried out with an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. The N2 adsorption and desorption tests were carried out by an ASAP-2020 specific surface area and aperture analyzer. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was conducted on a field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi S-4300). The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was characterized by transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100).

In this paper, electrochemical measurement of supercapacitors based on N-GQDs/rGOs samples was carried out in a two-electrode system. Typically, a slice of N-GQDs/rGOs hydrogel was directly pressed on the nickel foam, and then press it under 10 MPa for 0.5 min to make the working electrode. Afterward, the working electrode was activated overnight in 6 M KOH electrolyte. Finally, two nearly identical working electrodes and cellulose membranes were packaged into a 2025 type coin cell and assembled into a symmetrical supercapacitor.

The assembled supercapacitor was tested by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) which were performed on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation. The specific capacitances (C
s ) of the supercapacitors were calculated from the galvanostatic discharge curves based on equations C
s = 2(I × Δt/m × ΔV), where I, Δt, m and ΔV are the charge/discharge current (A), the discharge time (s), the mass in a single electrode (g) and the voltage range upon discharging without the IR drop (V), respectively. The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) were calculated based on equations E = C
s
ΔV
2
/8 and P = E/Δt, respectively.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was utilized to determine the crystal phases of GO, rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a, GO displays a strong peak at 2θ = 11.52° (001), with a d spacing of 0.77 nm. This d spacing is obviously larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm), demonstrating the high oxidation state of GO [25]. In the XRD spectra of rGO and N-GQDS/rGOs samples, the characteristic peak of GO disappeared and the characteristic peak of graphite appeared at about 24.3° (002), indicating that after hydrothermal process with ammonia and GQDs, the graphitized structure of the samples was restored and the samples were successfully reduced [26]. Compared to rGO, the XRD patterns of N-GQDS/rGOs have larger peak width, which means the defects caused by nitrogen doping and the introduction of GQDs. These defects introduced in the graphene skeleton can increase the electronic density of states at the Fermi level, which will make the material generate more quantum capacitances [27].

Raman spectroscopy of GO, rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs were presented in Fig. 1b. It is observed that all samples show two peaks, which can be assigned to D band ascribed to the defects and disordered sites in carbon materials and G band attributed to the graphite structure. As shown in Table 1 , the D band and G band of N-GQDs/rGOs samples are located at around 1344 and 1584 cm−1, which are similar to the nitrogen-doped GQDs synthesized by the hydrothermal approach [28]. Additionally, the peak positions of the D and G band s of the N-GQDs/rGOs samples are significantly shifted down relative to the GO and rGO samples, indicating that the sample has been effectively reduced, meanwhile, nitrogen atoms and graphene quantum dots have also successfully entered into the graphene lattice and combined with rGO [29]. Moreover, the I
D
/I
G values of N-GQDs/rGOs are apparently higher than that of rGO and GO samples. This phenomenon suggests that the nitrogen doping and composite GQDs with graphene sheets can produce more surface/edge defects in N-GQDs/rGOs samples, which can provide more electrochemical active sites for the materials and improve their electrochemical performance [30].

FT-IR spectra were utilized to examine the functional groups present in GO, rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs. As illustrated in Fig. 1c, the characteristic peaks of GO centered at about 3418, 1729, 1616, 1378, and 1060 cm−1 can be assigned to the O–H group originate from physisorbed water, CO stretching vibration of carbonyl moieties, CO stretching vibration in carboxyl, C–OH stretching vibration of hydroxyl and C–O vibration of epoxy, respectively [31]. After the hydrothermal reaction, the characteristic peak intensity of oxygen-containing functional groups in rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs is decreased compared to GO, verifying the incomplete reduction of GO (Figs. S1–S4) [32,33]. The retained oxygen-containing functional groups can not only maintain the hydrophilia of the sample, but also boost the pseudocapacitance of the sample through the Faraday redox reaction. Compared with GO and rGO samples, N-GQDs/rGOs display two diminutive peaks at around 1065 and 1570 cm−1 arise from the stretching vibration peak of C–N and N–H group, respectively. This indicates that nitrogen atoms have been successfully doped into N-GQDs/rGOs samples.

The C1s peak of N-GQDs/rGO-20 can be split into five sub-peaks located at around 284.7 (C–C/CC), 285.5 (C–OH), 286.4 (C–O/CN), 287.4 (CO/C–N) and 288.7 eV (O–CO), respectively (Fig. 2e). The appearance of C–N and CN further verifies the successful doping of nitrogen atoms in N-GQDs/rGOs samples. The fitted N1s spectra of N-GQDs/rGO-20 display four components at 398.5, 399.8, 400.5 and 401.4 eV, which can be ascribed to the pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 2f). These bonding configurations of N atoms in the N-GQDs/rGO-20 sample are consistent with the XPS analysis results of other nitrogen-doped graphene materials [36–41]. These nitrogen-containing functional groups can not only increase the electrical conductivity and hydrophilicity of the material, but also generate pseudocapacitance through the Faraday redox reaction [42].

The BET surface area and pore structure of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs samples were characterized by nitrogen adsorption-desorption tests (Fig. 3 ). As presented in Fig. 3a, all samples show type IV isotherm with an obvious hysteresis loop at the middle pressure region (0.4–0.9 P/P0), indicating the existence of mesopores. Moreover, the slightly increased at the low pressure region (P/P0 < 0.1) and high pressure region (P/P0 > 0.9) demonstrate the existence of micropores and macropores, respectively [43]. According to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) approach, the specific surface area of rGO, N-GQDs/rGO-10, N-GQDs/rGO-20 and N-GQDs/rGO-30 is 106.9, 247.3, 361.2 and 312.0 m2 g−1, respectively. Apparently, the N-GQDs/rGOs samples have higher specific surface area than that of rGO, which can be ascribed to the nitrogen doping and the introduction of GQDs between the graphene sheets. Most importantly, the larger specific surface area can make the N-GQDS/rGOs samples absorb more electrolyte ions on their surface and thus generate more EDLC capacitance. Fig. 3b shows the BJH pore size distribution of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs samples. It can be seen that the N-GQDs/rGOs samples present some micropores, abundant mesoporous and a small number of macropores. The co-existence of these pore structures is very helpful to improve the electrochemical performance of the N-GQDs/rGOs samples. Because micropores facilitate charge storage, mesopores can overcome the limitations of ion dynamics in electrochemical processes, and macropores can be used as ion buffer reservoirs [44]. The BET analyses verify that the N-GQDs/rGOs have high BET surface area and good porous structure, which will make them to be a good electrode material for supercapacitors.

The morphology and porous structure of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs samples were described by SEM and TEM measurements with freeze-dried samples (Fig. 4 ). It can be seen from Fig. 4a that rGO sample has a 3D porous structure, however, a large number of graphene sheets are stacked together, and most of the pores are blocked. This will make the electrolyte cannot fully infiltrate the sample, resulting in a low specific surface area utilization rate, which will seriously affect the electrochemical performance of the sample. Fig. 4b–d shows the 3D porous structure of N-GQDs/rGOs samples. Compared with rGO, the N-GQDs/rGOs samples have more voids with the pore size ranging from sub-micron to more than ten microns. This is because the GQDs added during the preparation process of N-GQDs/rGOs samples effectively inhibited the stacking and agglomeration of graphene sheets, which increased the distance between graphene sheets, and produced more pore structures [45]. These pores can provide more available channels for the rapid flow of electrolyte ions, and then boosting the rate performance of supercapacitors. In addition, more pore structure can also improve the utilization ratio of specific surface area of the samples, so as to increase their reactive sites, and finally improve their EDLC capacitance and pseudocapacitance.

Fig. 5 exhibits the TEM image of the samples. As exhibited in Fig. 5a–b, the GQDs have a typical spherical shape with a uniform size distribution of 2.0–4.6 nm, and the average diameter is about 3.5 nm. As shown in Fig. 5c, the graphene sheets in the rGO sample are severely agglomerated and stacked, which is agreed with the results of SEM. In the TEM diagram of N-GQDs/rGO-20 samples, it can be clearly seen that the GQDs are homogeneously distributed in the graphene sheets, with the diameter ranging from 30 to 40 nm, which is much larger than the pristine GQDs (Fig. 5d). We believe that this phenomenon can be attributed to the addition of ammonia water in the synthesis process of N-GQDs/rGOs, which adjusted the pH value of the reaction system to 10. Under such an alkaline system, GQDs will agglomerate, resulting in a larger particle size. Therefore, when GQDs are used as spacers, the distance between adjacent graphene sheets will be greatly increased, thereby broadening the transport channels of electrolyte ions.

The electrochemical properties of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs based binder-free symmetric supercapacitors were evaluated in 6 M KOH electrolyte with the potential window of 0–1V. Fig. 6 a exhibits the CV curves of N-GQDs/rGO-20 based symmetric supercapacitors at different scan rates. It is obvious that all CV curves of N-GQDs/rGO-20 display distorted rectangular shapes, suggesting the coexistence of EDLC capacitance and pseudocapacitance [46]. The EDLC capacitance of N-GQDS/rGO-20 sample can be ascribed to its large specific surface area, while the pseudocapacitance can be assigned to the reversible Faraday redox reaction of the abundant heteroatomic functional groups (N, O) on its surface. Furthermore, the shape of these CV curves maintains very stable, and there is no significant deformation even at 100 mV s−1, indicates that the N-GQDs/rGO-20 sample has good rate capability and reversibility [47]. Fig. 6b compares the CV curves of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs samples at 50 mV s−1. Obviously, the N-GQDs/rGOs samples have larger CV integral area than that of rGO, denoting their better capacitance performance. This is because the N-GQDs/rGOs possess higher specific surface area, better pore size structure, and increased conductivity and pseudocapacitance due to nitrogen doping.

Most importantly, the introduced GQDs not only improve the porous structure of the N-GQDs/rGOs samples and then increases the EDLC capacitance, but also boost the pseudocapacitance of the sample through the heteroatom functional groups on their edge. Moreover, the synergistic effect between GQDs and graphene sheets can facilitate the charge transfer of the N-GQDs/rGOs samples, and the defects introduced by GQDs can generate more quantum capacitances [48].

Fig. 6c presents the GCD profiles of the N-GQDs/rGO-20 electrodes at various current densities. Apparently, the GCD profiles of the N-GQDs/rGO-20 electrode show a quasi-triangular shape with a minor deformation in slopes, further proving the synergistic contribution of EDLC capacitance and pseudocapacitance in N-GQDs/rGO-20 sample. Furthermore, the GCD profiles at various current densities display negligible IR drops, suggesting a low charge transfer resistance and favorable discharging efficiency of N-GQDs/rGO-20 electrode. Fig. 6d exhibits the GCD profiles of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs electrodes at 1 A g−1. It can be seen that all of the N-GQDs/rGOs electrodes display longer discharge time compare with rGO demonstrate their better energy storage capacity [49], which is consistent with the CV results.

The specific capacitances of rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs electrodes based on the GCD curves are plotted in Fig. 7 a. As exhibited in Fig. 7a, with the increase of current density, the specific capacitance of all samples decreases gradually. This can be attributed to the rapid charge/discharge process that directly leads to a decrease in the adsorption capacity of the porous structure [46]. Obviously, the specific capacitances of the N-GQDs/rGOs electrodes are much larger than that of rGO. Especially, the N-GQDs/rGO-20 electrode shows high specific capacitances of 277.8, 267.2, 259.6, 251.1, 236.9 and 220.9 F g−1 at 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 and 10 A g−1, respectively. In comparison, the rGO electrode barely presents relatively low specific capacitances of 168.2, 165.3, 160.7, 155.2, 144.5 and 133.6 F g−1, respectively, at the same current densities. This suggests that the N-GQDs/rGOs samples combine the high conductivity of graphene sheet, the pseudocapacitance generated by heteroatomic functional group and the high electroactive of the GQDs, which greatly increases their specific capacitance.

The energy density and power density of the rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs based binder-free symmetric supercapacitors calculated from GCD curves are depicted in 7b. Based on the Ragone plots, the N-GQDs/rGO-20 based supercapacitor can delivers a high energy density of 9.6 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 74.9 W kg−1 at 0.3 A g−1. When the power density increase to 2352.3 W kg−1 the energy density can still reach up to 6.8 Wh kg−1 at 10 A g−1, verifying the excellent rate capability of the N-GQDs/rGO-20 electrode. Nevertheless, the rGO based supercapacitor can merely deliver lower energy densities of 5.8 and 3.8 Wh kg−1 at 74.5 and 2271.0 W kg−1, respectively. The results of Ragone analysis show that the N-GQDs/rGO-20 sample has a high energy density, which is beneficial to its practical applications.

EIS was carried out to investigate the electron and ion transport in the rGO and N-GQDs/rGOs electrodes. As shown in Fig. 7c, N-GQDs/rGOs electrodes display steeper slopes in the low frequency region than that of rGO, suggesting their ideal EDLC characteristics and lower diffusive resistance [50]. At the high frequency region, the N-GQDs/rGOs electrodes have smaller semicircle and intercept at the real axis compare to rGO (inset in Fig. 7c), which demonstrates the smaller charge transfer resistance and lower equivalent series resistance of N-GQDs/rGOs [51–53]. Moreover, the Warburg lines of N-GQDs/rGOs in the middle frequency region are much shorter compare with rGO electrode, denoting the higher ion diffusion rate in N-GQDs/rGOs [54]. The equivalent circuit model and related data are displayed in Fig. S5 and Table S1. Obviously, the bulk solution resistance (Rs) and charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of N-GQDs/rGO are smaller compare to that of rGO. The EIS results can be ascribed to the introduction of GQDs between the graphene sheets. The electrochemical activity of GQDs can promote the electron transfer in N-GQDs/rGOs, thus reducing their internal resistance and charge transfer resistance [55]. Furthermore, these results can also be ascribed to the large specific surface area, 3D porous structure and nitrogen doping of N-GQDs/rGOs samples, which will facilitate the transfer of electrolyte ions within the electrode material.

The difference in electrochemical performance of N-GQDs/rGOs samples can be attributed to the following reasons. (1) When the amount of GQDs is too much (N-GQDs/rGO-10), strong agglomeration will occur between GQDs in the hydrothermal process. This will cause GQDs to lose part of the spacer function and damage the specific surface area and porous structure of the material, thereby reducing the electrochemical performance of the sample. In addition, the agglomeration of GQDs will also reduce the pseudocapacitance performance of the sample. (2) When the amount of GQDs is too small (N-GQDs/rGO-30), the function of the spacer cannot be fully exerted, and the agglomeration of graphene sheets during the hydrothermal process will become relatively serious. At this time, the specific surface area and porous structure of the sample will also be affected, resulting in a bad influence on the electrochemical performance of the sample.

The cycle life and capacitance retention are important for real applications of supercapacitors. The cycle life of the N-GQDs/rGO-20 based symmetric supercapacitor was examined by GCD cycles at 10 A g−1. As seen in Fig. 7d, the symmetrical device retained 92.7% of its original specific capacitance after 10,000 GCD cycles, suggesting the outstanding cycle permanence of the N-GQDs/rGO-20 electrode. This proves that the GQDs have good affinity with graphene sheets [56], which suggests that the N-GQDs/rGO-20 electrode has great potential for real applications.

In summary, N-GQDs/rGOs electrode materials have been synthesized through hydrothermal method using GO as precursors, GQDs as spacer and ammonia water as reducing agent, pH regulator and nitrogen dopant. Due to the 3D porous structures, large specific surface area, abundant heteroatom functional groups (N, O) on the surface, and the synergistic effect between GQDs and graphene sheets, the N-GQDs/rGOs present excellent electrochemical performance. Especially, the N-GQDs/rGO-20 based binder-free symmetric supercapacitor exhibits high specific capacitance (277.8 F g−1 at 0.3 A g−1), good rate performance (79.5%), high energy density (9.6 Wh kg−1 at 74.9 W kg−1) and excellent cycle stability (92.7% capacitance retention after 10,000 GCD cycles) at the same time. Based on the above results, we think that the introduction of GQDs into graphene sheets can greatly boost the electrochemical properties of the materials, and has great practical value in the field of supercapacitors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support by the Qiqihar University Graduate Innovation Fund Grants (YJSCX2019041, YJSCX2020003), the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (135209201).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2023.128365.
